data.body:
PASS-1 REVIEW (criterion G6 — approach soundness / fitness-for-purpose). Two grounded findings for author consideration; the chosen approach (a gate-specific doc that cross-references the canonical docs/workflow-engine.md rather than duplicating it) is otherwise sound and defensibly best — no superior structural alternative, so approach-selection PASSES.

FINDING 1 (fitness-for-purpose; terminology error that would be documented verbatim):
The plan calls the enable/disable mechanism a "kill-switch" in both the What ("the global enable/disable kill-switch") and the first AC ("config (incl. the disable kill-switch)"). The parent epic 5fd2 EXPLICITLY settled the opposite: SC#4 reads "'rollback' is simply turning the preference off — an ordinary config preference, NOT a deploy-time kill-switch, and no separate rollback mechanism is needed." Since this is a docs task whose stated Why is honest documentation for a broad client base, documenting an ordinary opt-in config preference AS a "kill-switch" would mislead clients into expecting a deploy-time/rollback safety mechanism the gate deliberately does not have (and the default is OFF, which the plan also omits). Coaching: align the doc to the epic's settled semantics — describe it as a config enable/disable PREFERENCE that DEFAULTS OFF, not a kill-switch; reconcile or drop the "kill-switch" wording.

FINDING 2 (fitness-for-purpose; the doc must convey the honest cert semantics the epic flags as a correctness concern):
The plan lists "fail-open behavior" and "certification semantics" as separate topics but does not require the doc to CONNECT them. The epic treats this linkage as a real, G6/E6-class correctness concern: the round-5 critical review's "hollow signal — fail-open + advisory + --force certifies skipped/ignored" finding maps to G6, and the resolution (Design decisions revisited #2) is that the cert means "process followed, no red flags WITH coverage recorded — NOT 'perfect'." For the doc to be fit for its honest-documentation purpose, the cert-semantics section must state the bounded claim explicitly (what the signature does and does NOT attest, given fail-open coverage gaps, advisory-by-default non-blocking, and --force override) — otherwise a client reading the CI-rigor-signal section could over-trust the signature. The plan's existing "honest end-to-end time-to-first-work" AC shows the right instinct; recommend an analogous explicit honesty requirement on the cert-semantics + fail-open linkage. (Note: criteria[]=G6; severity/priority deliberately omitted per pass-1 protocol.)
